channel.title: Opportunities for Youth
channel.description: Empowering Youth through Access to Information and Opportunities

item.title: Luxembourg Working Student Program at JPMorganChase | 2026 Virtual Event
item.dc:creator: Susan Kamene
item.description:
The JPMorgan Chase Global Career Event is a prestigious professional networking and recruitment opportunity organized by one of the world’s leading financial institutions, JPMorgan Chase & Co.. These events are designed to introduce talented students and emerging professionals to the company’s career opportunities while providing insider knowledge about the financial services industry. Through this event, […]

The post Luxembourg Working Student Program at JPMorganChase | 2026 Virtual Event appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The JPMorgan Chase Global Career Event is a prestigious professional networking and recruitment opportunity organized by one of the world’s leading financial institutions, JPMorgan Chase & Co.. These events are designed to introduce talented students and emerging professionals to the company’s career opportunities while providing insider knowledge about the financial services industry.

Through this event, participants gain valuable insights into the company’s businesses, culture, and recruitment process while interacting directly with recruiters, industry professionals, and recent graduates working at the firm. The event also serves as a gateway to internships, graduate programs, and full-time employment opportunities within the organization.

As a global leader in investment banking, financial services, and asset management, JPMorgan Chase actively seeks the best talent worldwide to support its operations and innovation across markets. The company operates globally and serves millions of customers and institutional clients through its broad range of financial products and services.

Overview of the JPMorgan Career Networking Event

This event is a professional conference and recruitment event designed to connect potential candidates with JPMorgan’s global teams.

Participants who attend the event will gain:

  • Insight into the company’s core businesses and global operations

  • Career guidance from JPMorgan professionals

  • Networking opportunities with recruiters and employees

  • Information about internship programs and graduate recruitment

  • Tips on application processes, interviews, and career preparation

These sessions are often hosted virtually or at selected global locations and may include presentations, panel discussions, Q&A sessions, and networking opportunities with company representatives.

Such recruitment events play a major role in helping candidates understand how their skills, interests, and academic backgrounds can translate into a career in finance, technology, consulting, or business operations.

About JPMorgan Chase & Co.

JPMorgan Chase & Co. is one of the largest and most influential financial services companies in the world. Headquartered in New York, the firm provides a wide range of services including:

  • Investment banking

  • Consumer banking

  • Commercial banking

  • Asset and wealth management

  • Financial transaction processing

The company manages trillions of dollars in assets and serves corporations, governments, and individuals across the globe. With extensive global operations and a strong reputation in finance, JPMorgan Chase offers career opportunities in numerous sectors including technology, finance, risk management, data science, and consulting.

For aspiring professionals, participating in a JPMorgan career event provides direct exposure to one of the most respected employers in the financial industry.

What Participants Will Learn at the Event

Attending the JPMorgan career event allows participants to explore several important topics related to career development and the global financial industry.

1. Understanding JPMorgan’s Global Business Lines

Participants will learn about major divisions within the company such as:

  • Corporate & Investment Banking

  • Global Markets

  • Asset & Wealth Management

  • Consumer & Community Banking

  • Technology and Digital Innovation

These sessions help candidates understand the company’s structure and how different teams contribute to global financial markets.

2. Career Opportunities at JPMorgan

The event will highlight several entry points into the company, including:

  • Summer internships

  • Graduate programs

  • Full-time analyst roles

  • Early-career rotational programs

Recruiters often provide guidance on how applicants can transition from university into competitive financial careers.

3. Recruitment Process Insights

One of the most valuable aspects of this event is the opportunity to learn about the company’s recruitment process.

Participants will receive guidance on:

  • Preparing strong resumes and cover letters

  • Navigating online applications

  • Succeeding in technical and behavioral interviews

  • Developing networking strategies

Understanding these processes significantly increases applicants’ chances of success when applying to JPMorgan programs.

4. Networking with Industry Professionals

Attendees will also have the opportunity to interact directly with:

  • JPMorgan recruiters

  • Recent graduates working at the firm

  • Senior professionals in banking and finance

Networking sessions allow participants to ask questions about career paths, workplace culture, and skill development.

Who Can Apply for the JPMorgan Career Event

This opportunity is designed primarily for students and early-career professionals.

Eligible Categories of Applicants

Applicants who qualify typically include:

  • Undergraduate students

  • Master’s degree students

  • MBA candidates

  • Recent graduates

  • Early-career professionals seeking roles in finance or related fields

These events are particularly beneficial for individuals interested in:

  • Investment banking

  • Financial markets

  • Corporate finance

  • Risk management

  • Technology and fintech

  • Consulting and strategy

Educational Qualifications

While specific requirements vary by event, typical qualifications include:

  1. Enrollment in a university degree program or recent graduation.

  2. Academic background in fields such as:

    • Finance

    • Economics

    • Business administration

    • Accounting

    • Engineering

    • Computer science

    • Mathematics or statistics

  3. Strong analytical and communication skills.

  4. Interest in global financial markets and business strategy.

Importantly, many JPMorgan career events welcome students from all academic disciplines, not only finance majors.

Countries and Regions Eligible to Apply

The JPMorgan career event is typically open to applicants from multiple regions around the world, depending on the specific event.

Eligible applicants often come from:

Europe, Middle East and Africa (EMEA)

Countries across:

  • Africa

  • Middle Eastern nations

  • European countries

Asia-Pacific

Including applicants from:

  • Southeast Asia

  • East Asia

  • South Asia

  • Oceania

North and South America

Participants may also join from:

  • United States

  • Canada

  • Latin American countries

Because many events are virtual, students from nearly any country may be able to participate depending on registration eligibility.

Key Benefits of Attending the Event

Participating in the JPMorgan career networking event offers several advantages for aspiring professionals.

1. Early Access to Career Opportunities

Attendees often gain early access to internship and graduate program applications.

2. Insider Knowledge

Participants learn directly from JPMorgan employees about:

  • Industry trends

  • Career pathways

  • Skills required in global finance

3. Professional Networking

The event provides opportunities to connect with recruiters and professionals who may later become mentors or hiring managers.

4. Competitive Advantage

Students who attend such events often gain a competitive advantage in future job applications because they better understand what the company is looking for.

How to Apply for the JPMorgan Career Event

Applicants must complete an online registration through the JPMorgan recruitment platform.

Typical steps include:

  1. Visiting the official event registration page.

  2. Creating a candidate profile on the recruitment platform.

  3. Submitting personal information and academic background.

  4. Uploading a resume or CV.

  5. Confirming event participation.

Successful applicants may receive additional information about the event agenda and access instructions before the event date.

Apply here

Event date: 27th March 2026 (1:00pm – 2:30pm) GMT + 01:00 Brussels

Tips for a Successful Application

To maximize your chances of attending the event and benefiting from it, consider the following tips:

1. Prepare a Strong Resume

Highlight:

  • Academic achievements

  • Leadership roles

  • Internships or projects

  • Technical skills

2. Demonstrate Interest in Finance

Recruiters value candidates who show genuine interest in:

  • Global markets

  • Economic trends

  • Financial innovation

3. Engage Actively During the Event

Ask thoughtful questions, participate in discussions, and connect with professionals during networking sessions.

Why You Should Not Miss This Opportunity

The JPMorgan career networking event offers a rare opportunity to engage directly with one of the world’s most influential financial institutions.

For students and early-career professionals, attending this event can:

  • Provide valuable career insights

  • Expand professional networks

  • Increase chances of securing internships or full-time roles

  • Offer exposure to global financial industry practices

Because JPMorgan continues to recruit talented individuals across its global operations, participating in such events is often the first step toward building a career in international finance.

For more information visit here

For more global opportunities click here

The post Luxembourg Working Student Program at JPMorganChase | 2026 Virtual Event appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: New York Women’s Foundation Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant
item.dc:creator: Susan Kamene
item.description:
The Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant offered by The New York Women’s Foundation is a strategic funding opportunity designed to support grassroots organizations and community-based initiatives working to advance gender, racial, and economic justice. This grant program is part of the foundation’s long-standing commitment to investing in innovative, community-driven solutions that uplift women, girls, and […]

The post New York Women’s Foundation Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant offered by The New York Women’s Foundation is a strategic funding opportunity designed to support grassroots organizations and community-based initiatives working to advance gender, racial, and economic justice. This grant program is part of the foundation’s long-standing commitment to investing in innovative, community-driven solutions that uplift women, girls, and gender-expansive individuals.

Founded in 1987, the foundation has become one of the leading women-led philanthropic institutions globally, focusing on funding early-stage organizations and initiatives that address systemic inequalities affecting marginalized communities. The Early Investment Grant specifically targets emerging or under-resourced organizations whose work promotes social change and empowerment.

This funding opportunity is particularly valuable because it provides not only financial support but also credibility and long-term partnership opportunities. Many organizations funded by the foundation go on to receive additional investments and scale their impact significantly.

Overview of the Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant Program

The Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant is designed to fund organizations that are at an early stage of development but demonstrate strong potential to create transformative change within their communities.

The program emphasizes participatory grantmaking. This means that funding decisions are guided by community members, activists, and individuals with lived experiences related to the issues being addressed. This model ensures that resources are directed to the initiatives most capable of generating meaningful impact.

Through this approach, the foundation aims to:

  • Support grassroots leadership

  • Empower communities affected by inequality

  • Promote gender justice and economic equity

  • Build sustainable and scalable solutions

  • Strengthen local organizations serving marginalized populations

Organizations selected for the grant will receive funding for a one-year period and may also gain access to networking opportunities, capacity-building resources, and collaborative partnerships.

Grant Timeline and Key Dates

Applicants must carefully observe the timeline to ensure they do not miss important stages in the application process.

Important Dates for the Spring 2026 Grant Cycle

  • Information Session: March 5, 2026 (2:00 PM EST)

  • Application Deadline: 23rd March 2026

  • Site Visit Invitations Sent: 3rd April 2026

  • Site Visit Period: 13th April – 1st May 2026

  • Funding Decision Notification: 1st June 2026

  • Grant Implementation Period: 1st July 2026 – 30th June 2027

Applicants are strongly encouraged to participate in the information session to better understand the program requirements and evaluation criteria.

Who Can Apply for the NYWF Spring 2026 Grant

The grant is specifically designed for organizations that serve communities experiencing economic, racial, and gender-based inequalities.

Eligible applicants include:

  • Grassroots nonprofit organizations

  • Community-based organizations

  • Advocacy groups

  • Social justice initiatives

  • Women-led and gender-inclusive organizations

  • Emerging organizations addressing community challenges

The foundation prioritizes organizations that work directly with:

  • Women and girls

  • Gender-expansive individuals

  • Immigrants

  • LGBTQI+ communities

  • Low-income families

  • People with disabilities

More than 95% of the foundation’s funding supports organizations serving low- to moderate-income communities, reflecting its commitment to equity and inclusion.

Geographic Eligibility: Countries or Regions That Can Apply

This opportunity has a specific geographic focus.

Eligible applicants must:

  • Operate within the United States

  • Serve communities located within the five boroughs of New York City

These boroughs include:

  • Manhattan

  • Brooklyn

  • Queens

  • The Bronx

  • Staten Island

Organizations outside these areas are generally not eligible unless their work directly impacts communities within these boroughs.

Organizational Eligibility Requirements

To qualify for the Early Investment Grant, organizations must meet several basic criteria.

Legal Status Requirements

Applicants must:

  • Be a registered 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, or

  • Have a 501(c)(3) fiscal sponsor

This requirement ensures that funded organizations comply with nonprofit regulations and can properly manage grant funds.

Organizational Development Stage

The program particularly supports emerging or early-stage organizations, including those that:

  • Have limited access to institutional funding

  • Are developing innovative solutions to community challenges

  • Are led by individuals with lived experience of the issues they address

The foundation frequently acts as one of the first institutional investors in the organizations it funds, helping them establish credibility and attract additional funding sources.

Key Focus Areas of the Grant

The Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant prioritizes initiatives addressing critical social issues affecting women and marginalized communities.

Key focus areas include:

1. Health Equity and Reproductive Justice – Programs that expand healthcare access, reproductive rights, and health education for women and underserved populations.

2. Economic Justice – Initiatives aimed at improving financial stability, employment opportunities, and economic empowerment.

3. Safety and Healing Justice – Projects that address gender-based violence, trauma recovery, and community safety.

4. Expanding Democracy and Leadership – Programs that increase civic engagement, leadership development, and representation of marginalized groups.

What the Grant Supports

Funding from the Early Investment Grant can be used for a wide range of activities that strengthen organizations and their community programs.

These may include:

  • Launching new programs

  • Expanding existing initiatives

  • Community organizing and advocacy

  • Capacity-building and operational support

  • Leadership development

  • Outreach and community engagement

  • Strategic planning and program development

The foundation’s approach focuses on long-term sustainability, ensuring that funded organizations have the resources to continue their work beyond the grant period.

The Application Process

Applying for the Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant involves several steps designed to evaluate the organization’s impact and potential.

Step 1: Attend the Information Session

Organizations are encouraged to attend the March 5 information session to learn about the program guidelines and application process.

Step 2: Prepare the Application

Applicants must submit their proposals through the foundation’s online grant management platform.

Typical application materials may include:

  • Organizational background

  • Mission and program description

  • Community impact statement

  • Budget and funding plan

  • Leadership and governance details

Step 3: Application Submission

All applications must be submitted before the March 23, 2026 deadline.

Late submissions are typically not accepted.

Step 4: Site Visit

Selected organizations may be invited for a site visit between April 13 and May 1, 2026. These visits allow the foundation to learn more about the organization’s work and community impact.

Step 5: Final Decision

Successful applicants will receive official funding notifications by June 1, 2026.

Apply here

Benefits of Receiving the NYWF Early Investment Grant

Organizations that receive funding gain several advantages beyond financial support.

1. Financial Investment – Funding helps organizations expand programs and implement impactful initiatives.

2. Institutional Credibility – Being funded by a respected philanthropic organization strengthens an organization’s reputation and fundraising capacity.

3. Network and Partnerships – Grantees join a network of community leaders, activists, and organizations working toward shared goals.

4. Capacity Building – Grantees may receive training, mentorship, and strategic support that improves long-term organizational sustainability.

Why the New York Women’s Foundation Supports Early-Stage Organizations

The philosophy behind the Early Investment Grant is that community-led solutions are the most effective drivers of social change.

The foundation believes that people directly affected by social problems are best positioned to design solutions. By funding grassroots organizations early in their development, the foundation helps these initiatives grow into influential and sustainable movements.

This approach reflects a broader commitment to participatory philanthropy, where communities play a central role in decision-making about funding priorities.

Deadline

Applicants must ensure they submit their applications before the official deadline.

23rd March 2026

The Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant by the New York Women’s Foundation represents an important funding opportunity for organizations committed to advancing gender equity, racial justice, and economic empowerment. By investing in emerging grassroots organizations, the foundation continues to strengthen community leadership and drive meaningful social transformation. For organizations working to empower women, girls, and marginalized communities in New York City, this grant provides a powerful platform for growth, sustainability, and long-term impact.

For more information visit here

For more global opportunities click here

The post New York Women’s Foundation Spring 2026 Early Investment Grant appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Intercultural Innovation Hub 2026: Global Funding Opportunity for Non-Profit Organizations Advancing Social Inclusion and Intercultural Dialogue (Up to $20,000 Grant)
item.dc:creator: Susan Kamene
item.description:
Organizations around the world working to promote intercultural dialogue, social inclusion, and community cohesion now have a major opportunity to expand their impact. The Intercultural Innovation Hub 2026 has officially opened its global call for applications, inviting non-profit organizations to apply for financial support, mentorship, and capacity-building opportunities that can help scale their innovative social […]

The post Intercultural Innovation Hub 2026: Global Funding Opportunity for Non-Profit Organizations Advancing Social Inclusion and Intercultural Dialogue (Up to $20,000 Grant) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Organizations around the world working to promote intercultural dialogue, social inclusion, and community cohesion now have a major opportunity to expand their impact. The Intercultural Innovation Hub 2026 has officially opened its global call for applications, inviting non-profit organizations to apply for financial support, mentorship, and capacity-building opportunities that can help scale their innovative social initiatives.

The program is a collaboration between the United Nations Alliance of Civilizations, BMW Group, and Accenture, bringing together global expertise from international organizations, the private sector, and civil society. Together, these partners aim to strengthen innovative projects that foster dialogue among cultures, tackle prejudice, and build inclusive societies worldwide.

If your organization is working to address social tensions, promote diversity, or use innovative methods to foster understanding across communities, this program could provide the resources and global network needed to amplify your impact.

What Is the Intercultural Innovation Hub?

The Intercultural Innovation Hub (IIH) is a global initiative designed to identify and support innovative civil society projects that promote dialogue and cooperation between cultures. The program focuses on scaling social innovations that contribute to more inclusive societies and help address global challenges related to cultural misunderstanding and social division.

The hub acts as a platform for collaboration, funding, and capacity building. It brings together social innovators, non-profit organizations, and global experts to develop scalable solutions to complex intercultural issues.

Since its creation, the program has supported organizations that tackle topics such as discrimination, extremism, inequality, and cultural exclusion. Selected organizations gain access not only to financial resources but also to strategic mentoring and an international network of changemakers.

Funding and Benefits for Selected Organizations

Organizations selected for the Intercultural Innovation Hub will receive a comprehensive package designed to help them expand and strengthen their projects.

1. Financial Grant

Each finalist organization will receive up to USD $20,000 to support the expansion of its social innovation project. The funding is specifically intended to scale initiatives and increase their impact.

This financial support can help organizations:

  • Expand programs into new communities or regions

  • Strengthen project implementation

  • Increase beneficiary outreach

  • Improve sustainability and long-term impact

However, the funding is not intended for general operational costs such as salaries or office rent. Instead, it supports activities directly related to expanding the selected project.

2. One-Year Capacity-Building Program

Selected organizations participate in a year-long development program that includes:

  • Workshops and masterclasses

  • Mentorship from experts in innovation and social impact

  • Strategic guidance for scaling projects

  • Customized organizational support

These training sessions are designed to strengthen leadership skills, project management capacity, and innovation strategies.

3. Access to a Global Network

Another major advantage of the Intercultural Innovation Hub is membership in a global community of changemakers.

Organizations will gain opportunities to:

  • Connect with global experts and partners

  • Participate in international events and workshops

  • Collaborate with other organizations tackling similar challenges

  • Share knowledge and best practices across regions

This network helps organizations build partnerships that extend far beyond the grant period.

Key Focus Areas for Projects

The program is looking for innovative projects already making an impact in communities. Projects should address intercultural challenges and promote inclusion.

Examples of eligible focus areas include:

1. Combating Xenophobia and Hate Speech – Projects addressing discrimination, racism, and hate speech through education, advocacy, or digital tools.

2. Preventing Violent Extremism – Initiatives designed to counter radicalization and promote peaceful dialogue across communities.

3. Promoting Gender Equality – Programs empowering women and promoting equal participation in society.

4. Social Inclusion Through Arts, Sports, and Culture – Projects using creative or cultural activities to build bridges between communities.

5. Building Inclusive and Cohesive Societies – Initiatives strengthening community resilience and collaboration across diverse populations.

6. Using Technology for Social Good – Innovative uses of technology, including artificial intelligence, to promote intercultural understanding and human well-being.

Who Can Apply?

The Intercultural Innovation Hub is open to non-profit organizations worldwide that are already implementing projects related to intercultural dialogue and inclusion.

Eligible Applicants

Organizations that may apply include:

  • Non-governmental organizations (NGOs)

  • Charitable organizations

  • Research or educational institutions

  • Faith-based organizations

  • Professional associations

  • Foundations

  • Indigenous organizations

  • Labor unions

  • Local or municipal government branches

Applications are not open to individuals. Applicants must apply through a registered non-profit organization.

Eligible Countries and Regions

This opportunity is open globally, meaning organizations from any continent or country can apply.

Eligible regions include:

  • Africa

  • Asia

  • Europe

  • North and South America

  • Oceania

The program specifically encourages diverse participation to ensure global representation of intercultural initiatives.

Eligibility Requirements

To qualify for the Intercultural Innovation Hub 2026, organizations must meet several criteria.

Organizational Requirements

Applicants must:

  1. Be registered as a non-profit organization.

  2. Have been operational for at least two years prior to the application period.

  3. Demonstrate a clear track record of implementing social impact projects.

  4. Show commitment to diversity, inclusion, and intercultural dialogue.

Project Requirements

Projects submitted must:

  • Already be actively implemented and not just an idea.

  • Have demonstrated measurable impact during at least one year of operation.

  • Show potential for expansion or replication in other contexts.

  • Focus on intercultural dialogue and inclusive societies.

Projects that consist only of one-time events such as conferences or festivals are not eligible.

Selection Criteria

Applications are evaluated based on three major categories:

1. Project Quality

Reviewers will assess:

  • Relevance to social challenges

  • Innovation and originality

  • Sustainability of the approach

  • Measurable outcomes and impact

  • Potential for replication in other contexts

2. Organizational Strength

Evaluation includes:

  • Organizational structure and leadership

  • Transparency and accountability

  • Commitment to equality and diversity

  • Realistic project work plan and budget

3. Application Quality

Applications must clearly communicate:

  • Project objectives

  • Impact strategy

  • Implementation plan

  • Expected results and scalability

Organizations must present a compelling narrative and clear evidence of impact to increase their chances of selection.

Application Process

Organizations must apply online through the Intercultural Innovation Hub application platform.

The process typically includes the following steps:

  1. Submit an online application form.

  2. Eligibility screening by program organizers.

  3. Shortlisting of approximately 30 applicants.

  4. Final selection of 10 organizations by an international jury.

Finalists will then be invited to join the Intercultural Innovation Hub program and participate in the capacity-building activities.

Apply here

Why Organizations Should Apply

The Intercultural Innovation Hub offers far more than financial support. It provides a unique opportunity for social impact organizations to scale their work internationally.

Benefits include:

  • Access to funding for project expansion

  • Strategic mentorship from global experts

  • Capacity-building training

  • Visibility and recognition on an international stage

  • Opportunities to collaborate with global changemakers

For organizations dedicated to building inclusive societies and addressing cultural divisions, the program offers both resources and global exposure.

Deadline

15th April 2026 at 5:00 p.m. EDT (New York time). Late submissions will not be accepted.

The Intercultural Innovation Hub 2026 represents a powerful opportunity for non-profit organizations around the world to strengthen their social innovation initiatives. By combining funding, mentorship, and global collaboration, the program aims to accelerate impactful solutions that promote dialogue, inclusion, and mutual understanding across cultures.

For more information visit here

For more global opportunities click here

The post Intercultural Innovation Hub 2026: Global Funding Opportunity for Non-Profit Organizations Advancing Social Inclusion and Intercultural Dialogue (Up to $20,000 Grant) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Network & Membership Coordinator Job at UNOY: Work With a Global Youth Peacebuilding Network in The Hague
item.dc:creator: Maria Ramirez
item.description:
The United Network of Young Peacebuilders (UNOY) is seeking a Network & Membership Coordinator to help strengthen and expand its global youth peacebuilding network. Based in The Hague, Netherlands, this role offers a unique opportunity to support youth-led organizations working toward peaceful societies across more than 80 countries. This position is ideal for professionals passionate […]

The post Network & Membership Coordinator Job at UNOY: Work With a Global Youth Peacebuilding Network in The Hague appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The United Network of Young Peacebuilders (UNOY) is seeking a Network & Membership Coordinator to help strengthen and expand its global youth peacebuilding network. Based in The Hague, Netherlands, this role offers a unique opportunity to support youth-led organizations working toward peaceful societies across more than 80 countries.

This position is ideal for professionals passionate about locally led peacebuilding, youth engagement, and international network coordination. The successful candidate will play a key role in guiding the development of UNOY’s global network and ensuring strong collaboration between member organizations.

About UNOY

The United Network of Young Peacebuilders is a youth-led global network consisting of 100+ youth-led organizations operating in more than 80 countries. The network supports youth working in peacebuilding by facilitating collaboration, advocacy, capacity building, and knowledge exchange.

The organization’s International Secretariat is located in The Hague—the City of Peace and Justice—and is composed of a diverse international team of staff, interns, and regional coordinators.

UNOY has been implementing a decentralization strategy since 2017, transferring greater leadership and resources to regional structures. In 2026, the organization will develop a new roadmap to guide decentralization efforts through 2030.

Position Overview

The Network & Membership Coordinator will oversee network development, member engagement, and regional coordination across UNOY’s six global regions.

The role involves close collaboration with regional coordinators, staff members, partners, and donors to ensure effective communication and coordination within the network.

The selected candidate will also recruit and manage a Network Officer and support strategic decision-making within the organization.

Position Details

  • Position Title: Network & Membership Coordinator

  • Location: The Hague, The Netherlands

  • Workload: Part-time (0.8 FTE / 32 hours per week)

  • Start Date: Early May 2026

  • Office Requirement: At least 50% of working hours must be spent in the office

Key Responsibilities

1. Network Coordination and Development

  • Oversee the ongoing decentralization process of the global network

  • Coordinate and support the work of six Regional Coordinators

  • Assist with the development and monitoring of regional action plans

  • Facilitate International Steering Group meetings and elections

  • Strengthen coordination and accountability across different network bodies

2. Membership Engagement and Communication

  • Manage the annual membership application and disaffiliation process

  • Maintain and update UNOY’s membership database

  • Encourage active participation of member organizations

  • Facilitate communication across the network’s internal structures

  • Share opportunities and resources with member organizations

3. Project Implementation

  • Design and implement global and regional projects for network members

  • Lead the network’s peer-learning initiatives and collaborative activities

  • Monitor and evaluate project outcomes

4. Monitoring, Evaluation, and Organizational Learning

  • Contribute to organizational monitoring and evaluation processes

  • Support outcome harvesting and impact reporting

  • Represent UNOY at international conferences and advocacy events

  • Recruit, manage, and supervise the Network Officer

Requirements

Candidates should have:

  • A bachelor’s degree in a relevant field such as peace studies, international relations, development studies, or gender studies

  • At least two years of experience in peacebuilding, humanitarian work, development, or human rights

  • Experience in network coordination or community management

  • Strong relationship management and intercultural communication skills

  • Commitment to locally led peacebuilding, gender equity, and decolonization

  • Excellent English communication skills

Additional language skills such as French, Spanish, or Arabic are considered an advantage.

Benefits and Compensation

The position offers competitive benefits and opportunities for professional development within an international network.

Key benefits include:

  • Monthly gross salary of €2556 (0.8 FTE)

  • Annual salary growth potential

  • Holiday allowance equal to 8% of gross annual salary

  • 20 annual leave days plus Dutch public holidays

  • Free vegetarian lunches at the office

  • Work-from-home and commuting allowances

  • Annual training and professional development budget

  • Opportunities for international travel

  • Six weeks of remote working allowance per year

Important Eligibility Note

Applicants must already have legal authorization to work in the Netherlands. The organization cannot sponsor work permits or visas for this role.

How to Apply

Interested applicants must apply through the official UNOY application form and upload their CV along with answers to a few motivation and skills questions.

Applicants are encouraged to submit authentic responses in their application process.

Application Link:
https://unoy.org/jobs/

Application Deadline: 22 March 2026 at 23:59 CEST

APPLY HERE. 

For more information about this apportunity, click here. 

Shortlisted candidates will be invited for interviews scheduled between 26 March and 2 April 2026.

Discover more job opportunities, click here. 

The post Network & Membership Coordinator Job at UNOY: Work With a Global Youth Peacebuilding Network in The Hague appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund (CDAF) Small Grants Program 2026: Up to $10,000 for U.S. Exchange Alumni Addressing Global Challenges
item.dc:creator: Hadir Arfaoui
item.description:
The Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund (CDAF) Small Grants Program is now open for applications for its 2026 funding cycle, offering financial support to alumni of U.S. Government exchange programs who want to transform their international exchange experiences into impactful initiatives addressing local and global challenges. Administered as part of the U.S. Exchange Alumni Network and […]

The post Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund (CDAF) Small Grants Program 2026: Up to $10,000 for U.S. Exchange Alumni Addressing Global Challenges appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund (CDAF) Small Grants Program is now open for applications for its 2026 funding cycle, offering financial support to alumni of U.S. Government exchange programs who want to transform their international exchange experiences into impactful initiatives addressing local and global challenges.

Administered as part of the U.S. Exchange Alumni Network and Capacity Building Program, the CDAF Small Grants Program provides grants of up to $10,000 for collaborative projects led by exchange alumni teams. These projects are designed to strengthen communities, expand international partnerships, and amplify the long-term impact of U.S. Government exchange programs.

The program encourages alumni to use the knowledge, networks, and skills they developed during their exchange experiences to design projects that promote collaboration, innovation, and diplomacy. Through these initiatives, participants help strengthen international relationships while addressing pressing social, economic, cultural, and policy challenges.

Applications for the 2026 CDAF Small Grants Program opened on March 5, 2026, and the deadline for submission is May 5, 2026.

What Is the Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund?

The Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund was created to extend the impact of U.S. Government-sponsored exchange programs by empowering alumni to continue their engagement after completing their exchanges. Exchange programs funded by the U.S. government, such as leadership programs, cultural exchanges, and educational initiatives, create powerful networks of professionals and leaders across the world.

The CDAF program builds on these networks by supporting alumni-led projects that promote cooperation, diplomacy, and problem-solving across borders.

Through small grants and capacity-building opportunities, CDAF encourages alumni to implement projects that address real-world challenges while strengthening ties between communities in the United States and abroad.

These initiatives not only generate practical solutions but also highlight the value of international exchanges as a tool for fostering mutual understanding and global cooperation.

Funding Amount and Project Duration

The CDAF Small Grants Program offers funding for projects developed and implemented by teams of exchange alumni.

Key funding details include:

  • Maximum grant amount: Up to $10,000 per project

  • Project implementation period: Six months

Selected projects will be implemented between July and December 2026, following the announcement of successful applicants in June 2026.

During the project implementation period, participating teams will also benefit from structured support designed to help them manage and deliver their initiatives effectively.

Activities and Project Implementation

CDAF-funded projects are expected to apply the knowledge and skills gained through U.S. Government exchange programs to create meaningful and sustainable change.

Projects must be designed and implemented by teams of two or more exchange alumni, encouraging collaboration and knowledge sharing among members of the global alumni network.

Selected teams will:

  • Participate in project management training coursework

  • Engage in regular progress check-ins with IREX

  • Implement their proposed initiatives during a six-month project cycle

  • Present their results during the Virtual CDAF Showcase following project completion

The final showcase, scheduled for January 2027, provides an opportunity for teams to present their project outcomes, share lessons learned, and highlight the collective impact of alumni initiatives worldwide.

Priority Project Themes

All project proposals submitted to the Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund must address a challenge within one of the four thematic priority areas defined by the program.

Celebrating America’s 250th Birthday and Global Connections

Projects in this category explore the role of arts, sports, and cultural exchange in strengthening global connections and celebrating the upcoming 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence.

Advancing Free Speech and Expression

This theme supports initiatives that promote freedom of expression through creative and technological mediums such as arts, media, and digital platforms.

Projects may focus on strengthening media literacy, promoting creative expression, or protecting open dialogue.

Fostering Economic Prosperity

Proposals addressing this theme focus on promoting economic development and entrepreneurship. Projects may support:

  • English language education

  • Innovation initiatives

  • Business partnerships

  • Workforce development programs

These initiatives aim to contribute to sustainable economic growth and collaboration between communities.

Strengthening Alumni Networks

This theme focuses on strengthening relationships within the U.S. Government exchange alumni network and fostering collaboration that contributes to a safer, stronger, and more connected global community.

Projects may include networking initiatives, leadership development activities, or partnerships that enhance alumni engagement.

Application Requirements

Applicants must submit a complete application package that includes several required components. Incomplete applications submitted by the deadline will be considered ineligible.

All materials must be written in English.

Required documents include:

  • Completed application form

  • Detailed work plan

  • Project budget

  • Professional resumes of team members

  • Letter of intent from partner organizations (if applicable)

The work plan should clearly outline project objectives, activities, expected outcomes, and implementation strategies.

Applicants are also encouraged to demonstrate how their project builds upon the skills and experience gained during their exchange program.

Eligibility Requirements

To be considered for funding, applicants must meet the following eligibility criteria:

  • Projects must address one of the four CDAF thematic priorities.

  • Applications must be submitted by teams of at least two alumni of U.S. Government exchange programs.

  • Each team must designate two team leaders.

  • The primary team leader must be a U.S. citizen.

  • The second team leader may be either a U.S. citizen or an international citizen.

  • All team members must be alumni of U.S. Government-sponsored exchange programs.

  • All participants must be 18 years of age or older.

  • All team members must be alumni in good standing.

Employees of the U.S. Department of State, IREX, and their immediate family members are not eligible to participate.

Selection Criteria

Applications will be evaluated based on several key factors that measure the potential impact and feasibility of proposed projects.

Reviewers will assess proposals according to:

  • Project methodology and feasibility

  • Clear alignment with program themes

  • Measurable goals and outcomes

  • Qualifications and expertise of the applicant team

  • Evidence of collaborative leadership between team leaders

  • Reasonableness of the project budget

  • Potential for impact beyond the project’s duration

Projects conducted outside the United States must demonstrate a strong connection to the alumni’s exchange program and must be implemented in a country where the team leaders have substantial prior experience.

CDAF 2026 Timeline

The Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund follows a structured timeline for the 2026 funding cycle.

  • March 5, 2026: Application period opens

  • May 5, 2026: Application deadline

  • June 2026: Selected projects announced

  • July – December 2026: Project implementation period

  • January 2027: Virtual CDAF Showcase

Applicants should carefully plan their proposals to align with this schedule.

Information Sessions and Networking Opportunities

To support prospective applicants, the U.S. Exchange Alumni Program offers several informational events and networking sessions.

These sessions allow potential applicants to learn more about the program and identify possible collaborators for their project teams.

Upcoming sessions include:

  • CDAF Team Networking Session: March 26, 2026

  • CDAF Application Information Session: April 9, 2026

  • CDAF Application Virtual Office Hours: April 28, 2026

These events provide guidance on project design, application requirements, and collaboration opportunities.

Why the CDAF Program Matters

The Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund represents a powerful example of how international exchange programs can create lasting impact beyond the initial experience.

By empowering alumni to implement projects that address real-world challenges, CDAF strengthens diplomatic ties, promotes innovation, and encourages collaborative solutions across borders.

For alumni of U.S. Government exchange programs, this initiative offers a unique opportunity to translate their exchange experiences into meaningful community impact and global engagement.

For more information click here 

Discover more opportunities here 

The post Citizen Diplomacy Action Fund (CDAF) Small Grants Program 2026: Up to $10,000 for U.S. Exchange Alumni Addressing Global Challenges appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Global Social Impact House Fellowship 2026: Fully Funded Residential Program in Costa Rica for Social Entrepreneurs and Changemakers
item.dc:creator: Isaac O
item.description:
Applications are now open for the Global Social Impact House (GSIH) 2026, a prestigious residential fellowship program hosted by the Center for Social Impact Strategy (CSIS) at the University of Pennsylvania. This unique seven-day program is designed to support social entrepreneurs, innovators, and changemakers who are actively working to create positive social impact. The Global […]

The post Global Social Impact House Fellowship 2026: Fully Funded Residential Program in Costa Rica for Social Entrepreneurs and Changemakers appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications are now open for the Global Social Impact House (GSIH) 2026, a prestigious residential fellowship program hosted by the Center for Social Impact Strategy (CSIS) at the University of Pennsylvania. This unique seven-day program is designed to support social entrepreneurs, innovators, and changemakers who are actively working to create positive social impact.

The Global Social Impact House Fellowship brings together 20 global leaders working on impactful social initiatives and provides them with the tools, mentorship, and collaborative community needed to strengthen their ventures.

The 2026 edition of the program will take place September 12–18, 2026, at Macaw Lodge in Costa Rica. Participants will engage in intensive workshops, peer learning sessions, mentorship opportunities, and collaborative problem-solving activities in a serene and inspiring environment designed to foster creativity and reflection.

The Center for Social Impact Strategy will provide full program scholarships covering accommodation and meals, allowing fellows to focus entirely on learning and developing their social ventures.

Applicants must submit their applications by March 25, 2026.

About the Global Social Impact House Fellowship

The Global Social Impact House is an immersive learning experience designed for individuals leading or developing social impact initiatives.

The program focuses on helping participants:

  • Strengthen the strategy and sustainability of their ventures

  • Gain new tools and frameworks for social innovation

  • Connect with a global network of mission-driven leaders

  • Receive mentorship and feedback from experienced coaches

During the fellowship, participants live together in a shared environment that encourages deep collaboration, meaningful conversations, and personal reflection.

Rather than a traditional classroom program, GSIH emphasizes experiential learning, community building, and creative problem solving.

Key Features of the Global Social Impact House

The fellowship includes several components designed to support participants’ personal and professional development.

1. Advanced Social Impact Training

Participants will take part in interactive workshops led by experts in social innovation and impact strategy.

Topics covered include:

  • Human-centered design

  • Social enterprise business models

  • Leadership and team development

  • Storytelling for social impact

  • Strategic growth planning

  • Venture sustainability

Workshops are customized to address the specific needs and goals of the fellows.

2. Peer-to-Peer Learning and Collaboration

A core feature of the program is the collaborative environment created among fellows.

Participants will:

  • Share experiences from their ventures

  • Exchange ideas and feedback

  • Co-create solutions to complex challenges

  • Support one another’s professional development

The program encourages strong peer relationships that often continue long after the fellowship ends.

3. Mentorship from Leading Social Impact Experts

Throughout the program, fellows receive mentorship and guidance from experienced coaches and practitioners.

Mentors provide:

  • Personalized feedback on venture development

  • Strategic advice on scaling impact

  • Leadership coaching

  • Guidance on navigating social impact challenges

4. A Global Network of Changemakers

Participants become part of a global community of social innovators and leaders connected through the Center for Social Impact Strategy.

Benefits of joining this network include:

  • Access to a community of mission-driven leaders

  • Opportunities for collaboration and partnerships

  • Continued learning and knowledge exchange

  • Long-term professional connections

5. Digital Detox and Personal Reflection

One unique feature of the Global Social Impact House is its emphasis on intentional disconnection from technology.

Participants are encouraged to step away from daily digital distractions in order to:

  • Reconnect with their personal purpose

  • Reflect on their leadership journey

  • Focus deeply on their social ventures

  • Build authentic connections with other fellows

Program Location: Costa Rica

The 2026 Global Social Impact House will take place at Macaw Lodge, an eco-resort located in the San José Valley of Costa Rica.

Macaw Lodge is a 264-acre rainforest retreat designed as a model of environmental sustainability.

Features of the location include:

  • Off-grid facilities powered by solar energy

  • Lodging built from reclaimed and sustainably sourced materials

  • Access to tropical forests, rivers, and wildlife

  • Farm-to-table food and natural surroundings

Participants will have opportunities to relax and recharge between sessions while enjoying activities such as:

  • Birdwatching

  • Swimming in natural rivers

  • Yoga and meditation

  • Nature walks

  • Cultural experiences

The tranquil setting is intentionally chosen to support creativity, innovation, and reflection.

Program Schedule Overview

The seven-day fellowship program is carefully designed to balance learning, collaboration, and reflection.

Day 1: Orientation

Participants arrive at the retreat and begin building connections within the cohort.

Day 2: Community Building

Fellows share their personal journeys and ventures while building trust and community.

Day 3: Storytelling

Workshops focus on developing communication skills and articulating social impact stories.

Day 4: Design Thinking

Participants engage in human-centered design exercises to prototype solutions and refine ideas.

Day 5: Strategy Development

Fellows work with coaches and peers to strengthen their venture strategies.

Day 6: Peer-Led Workshops

Participants lead discussions on topics related to their expertise and experiences.

Day 7: Closing and Departure

The program concludes with reflections and action planning for future impact.

Financial Support and Program Costs

Participation in the Global Social Impact House Fellowship is fully funded by the Center for Social Impact Strategy.

The scholarship includes:

  • Full program participation

  • Accommodation at Macaw Lodge

  • All meals during the program

  • Access to workshops and mentorship sessions

Participants are responsible for covering:

  • Travel to and from San José, Costa Rica

  • Visa costs if required

  • Personal expenses during travel

Eligibility Requirements

Applicants must meet the following requirement before applying:

  • Complete the CSIS online course in Social Impact Strategy or another CSIS educational program.

Applicants should also:

  • Be actively working on a social impact project, organization, or enterprise

  • Demonstrate a strong commitment to creating social change

  • Be willing to participate in the full seven-day program

Priority will be given to applicants who are already working on a social venture with demonstrated impact or strong potential.

However, the program welcomes ventures at different stages, including:

  1. Early-stage concepts

  2. Pilot programs

  3. Scaling initiatives

How to Apply

Interested applicants should follow these steps:

  1. Complete the CSIS Social Impact Strategy online course or another CSIS program.

  2. Prepare information about your social venture and its impact.

  3. Submit your application through the Official Application Portal.

Applicants should clearly explain:

  • The social challenge their venture addresses

  • The impact their initiative aims to achieve

  • How participation in GSIH will strengthen their work

Application Deadline

Applications for the Global Social Impact House Fellowship 2026 must be submitted by March 25, 2026.

Decisions regarding selected fellows will not be announced before May 1, 2026.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

1. Is the Global Social Impact House Fellowship free?

Yes. The program is fully funded. Accommodation, meals, and program participation are covered.

However, fellows must pay for their own travel and visa expenses.

2. How long is the fellowship?

The program lasts 7 days, including arrival and departure.

Participants must attend the entire program.

3. Do I need to have an existing project?

Yes. Applicants must be working on a social impact initiative, enterprise, or organization.

Priority is given to those with an existing venture.

4. What stage should my venture be at?

The program accepts ventures at various stages:

  • Idea stage

  • Pilot stage

  • Scaling stage

The goal is to help fellows strengthen the viability and impact of their initiatives.

5. Is the program open to international applicants?

Yes. The Global Social Impact House is open to applicants from around the world.

Learn more about the Global Social Impact House Fellowship 2026 HERE.

Click HERE to discover more global Fellowship opportunities on OFY.

The post Global Social Impact House Fellowship 2026: Fully Funded Residential Program in Costa Rica for Social Entrepreneurs and Changemakers appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: BRIC TV Open Call 2026: Filmmakers Can Win Up to $15,000 to Produce Short Films and Pilots with National Endowment for the Arts Support
item.dc:creator: Hadir Arfaoui
item.description:
Filmmakers and storytellers are invited to submit their creative pitches to the BRIC TV Open Call 2026, an exciting opportunity designed to support emerging and independent creators in producing original scripted content. Supported by funding from the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA), this initiative offers financial backing, production support, and broadcasting opportunities for selected […]

The post BRIC TV Open Call 2026: Filmmakers Can Win Up to $15,000 to Produce Short Films and Pilots with National Endowment for the Arts Support appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Filmmakers and storytellers are invited to submit their creative pitches to the BRIC TV Open Call 2026, an exciting opportunity designed to support emerging and independent creators in producing original scripted content. Supported by funding from the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA), this initiative offers financial backing, production support, and broadcasting opportunities for selected projects.

Through this open call, four filmmaking projects will be selected to receive production and post-production support. Two projects will be produced as scripted short films, while two projects will be developed as television pilots. In addition to financial funding, selected creators will receive access to professional resources, equipment, and festival support to help their projects reach wider audiences.

This initiative reflects BRIC’s long-standing commitment to supporting artists, storytellers, and creative communities. For more than four decades, BRIC has played a significant role in shaping Brooklyn’s cultural landscape by providing resources, platforms, and opportunities for artists to share their work and voices.

Filmmakers interested in applying must submit their proposals by the official deadline of March 28, 2026 at 11:59 PM Eastern Time (ET).

About BRIC and BRIC TV

BRIC is a leading arts and media organization based in Brooklyn, New York, dedicated to supporting creative expression and cultural development. For more than forty years, BRIC has empowered artists, media makers, students, and cultural innovators through programs that promote storytelling, community engagement, and artistic experimentation.

BRIC TV serves as one of the organization’s major media platforms, broadcasting original content on noncommercial web and cable television channels. The network highlights diverse voices and innovative storytelling while offering creators opportunities to develop professional media productions.

Through initiatives such as the BRIC TV Open Call, the organization continues to support independent filmmakers and emerging storytellers by helping them transform creative ideas into professionally produced films and television content.

Funding Opportunities Available

The BRIC TV Open Call provides financial support for four selected filmmaking projects.

The funding distribution includes:

  • Two scripted short films will receive $15,000 each for production and post-production.

  • Two television pilot projects will receive $7,500 each.

These funds are intended to support key stages of production, including filming, editing, post-production, and final delivery of the project.

The program is funded in part by the National Endowment for the Arts, a federal agency that supports artistic excellence, creativity, and cultural development throughout the United States.

Additional Support for Selected Filmmakers

Beyond financial support, selected filmmakers will gain access to a variety of professional production resources and industry opportunities.

Successful applicants will receive:

  • Access to professional editing suites

  • Use of selected production equipment

  • Festival submission fee support

  • Guidance on festival marketing strategies

  • Broadcasting opportunities on BRIC’s web and cable television platforms

This comprehensive support helps filmmakers not only produce their work but also position it for greater visibility within the film festival circuit and media landscape.

Special Creative Theme: NYC History and Freedom 250

As part of this year’s open call, BRIC will give special consideration to one project that highlights the history of New York City in relation to the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence.

This initiative is part of the Freedom 250 celebration, which commemorates the historic event and explores the broader cultural and historical narratives surrounding American independence.

Projects that creatively explore New York City’s history, culture, or role in the nation’s development may receive additional attention during the selection process.

What BRIC Is Looking For

BRIC is seeking creative pitches that demonstrate strong storytelling, originality, and readiness for production.

Applicants are encouraged to submit projects that include:

  • Scripted short films

  • Scripted television pilots

The organization prioritizes proposals that demonstrate:

  • A strong creative vision

  • Clear storytelling and narrative structure

  • Production readiness

  • A well-developed creative team

  • The ability to deliver a completed project within the program timeline

Projects should reflect compelling storytelling and show the potential to engage audiences through innovative ideas and meaningful narratives.

Production Timeline

Selected projects must adhere to a production timeline that allows for the completion and submission of the final cut by October 31, 2026.

Filmmakers should ensure their project schedules account for all stages of production, including filming, editing, post-production, and final delivery.

Meeting this timeline is essential for the project to be broadcast through BRIC’s media platforms and potentially submitted to film festivals.

Submission Requirements

Applicants must submit a complete pitch package that clearly presents their project concept and production plan.

Required materials include:

  • A pitch deck

  • A detailed project budget

  • Cast and crew information

  • A film synopsis

  • Links to past work

  • Any additional relevant materials that support the proposal

The pitch deck should provide a comprehensive overview of the project, including the story concept, visual approach, creative team, and production strategy.

Including examples of previous work is strongly recommended, as it helps evaluators assess the applicant’s filmmaking style and experience.

How to Apply

Applications must be submitted by email.

Applicants should send their submission materials with the subject line:

“2026 BRIC NEA Pitch/[Project Name]”

Submissions must be received before the official deadline.

Application Deadline

The deadline for submissions is:

Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 11:59 PM Eastern Time (ET).

Late submissions will not be accepted.

Filmmakers are strongly encouraged to prepare their materials in advance to ensure their applications are submitted on time.

Why This Opportunity Matters for Filmmakers

The BRIC TV Open Call represents an important opportunity for filmmakers and storytellers to gain financial support, professional resources, and exposure through a respected cultural organization.

In addition to funding, the program offers valuable industry connections and opportunities to showcase creative work to wider audiences through television platforms and film festivals.

For emerging filmmakers and independent creators, this initiative provides the resources necessary to transform creative ideas into professional productions while contributing to the vibrant storytelling culture supported by BRIC.

For more information click here 

Discover more opportunities here 

The post BRIC TV Open Call 2026: Filmmakers Can Win Up to $15,000 to Produce Short Films and Pilots with National Endowment for the Arts Support appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: EOCA Conservation Project Funding Call 2026: Up to €30,000 for Global Biodiversity Protection and Community-Based Conservation Projects
item.dc:creator: Hadir Arfaoui
item.description:
The European Outdoor Conservation Association (EOCA) has officially launched its 2026 Conservation Project Funding Round, inviting non-profit organisations around the world to apply for financial support for impactful biodiversity conservation initiatives. This global funding opportunity supports practical, field-based conservation projects aimed at protecting threatened species, restoring ecosystems, and reconnecting communities with nature. Through this programme, […]

The post EOCA Conservation Project Funding Call 2026: Up to €30,000 for Global Biodiversity Protection and Community-Based Conservation Projects appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The European Outdoor Conservation Association (EOCA) has officially launched its 2026 Conservation Project Funding Round, inviting non-profit organisations around the world to apply for financial support for impactful biodiversity conservation initiatives. This global funding opportunity supports practical, field-based conservation projects aimed at protecting threatened species, restoring ecosystems, and reconnecting communities with nature.

Through this programme, EOCA provides grants of up to €30,000 per project to support conservation initiatives that can run for up to 24 months. The funding is designed to help organizations implement hands-on conservation activities that contribute to the protection and restoration of biodiversity while also addressing wider environmental challenges such as climate change and ecosystem degradation.

The EOCA Conservation Project Funding Call is widely recognized as an important source of financial support for grassroots conservation initiatives. By supporting projects in diverse ecosystems around the world, EOCA helps preserve critical habitats and empower communities to become active stewards of their natural environments.

Organizations interested in applying must submit their applications before the deadline of 31 March 2026 at 12:00 Midnight (BST / GMT+1). Late submissions will not be accepted.

About the European Outdoor Conservation Association (EOCA)

The European Outdoor Conservation Association is an organization created by members of the outdoor recreation industry to protect and conserve wild landscapes and natural ecosystems. EOCA supports projects that safeguard biodiversity, protect threatened wildlife species, and restore ecosystems that are essential for maintaining environmental balance.

Since its creation, EOCA has funded numerous conservation projects across the globe, working in partnership with environmental organizations, conservation groups, and local communities. The association places strong emphasis on practical conservation outcomes that directly improve environmental conditions and biodiversity protection.

By supporting conservation initiatives worldwide, EOCA contributes to global environmental sustainability while helping communities develop long-term solutions to environmental challenges.

Funding Amount and Project Duration

The EOCA Conservation Project Funding programme offers significant financial support for practical conservation initiatives.

Funding details include:

  • Maximum funding per project: Up to €30,000

  • Project duration: Up to 24 months

This funding enables organizations to carry out concrete conservation work that can produce measurable results within a two-year implementation period.

Projects should demonstrate clear conservation objectives, practical implementation strategies, and tangible environmental outcomes.

Focus Areas of EOCA Conservation Funding

EOCA prioritises projects that contribute to the protection and restoration of biodiversity in wild and natural landscapes. Projects should focus on ecosystems that play a critical role in environmental sustainability and climate resilience.

Eligible conservation focus areas include:

  • Forest conservation and restoration

  • Coastal and marine ecosystem restoration

  • Wetland protection and freshwater habitat conservation

  • Mangrove restoration initiatives

  • Rewilding and soil restoration projects

  • Protection of blue carbon ecosystems

  • Biodiversity protection in natural landscapes

These ecosystems are particularly important because they contribute to carbon sequestration, climate regulation, and biodiversity preservation.

By protecting and restoring these environments, EOCA-supported projects contribute to global efforts to combat climate change and reverse biodiversity loss.

Conservation and Climate Change

EOCA emphasises the strong connection between biodiversity conservation and climate action. Projects supported under this funding programme should demonstrate how conservation activities contribute to broader environmental resilience.

For example, conservation initiatives may focus on restoring ecosystems that store large amounts of carbon, such as mangrove forests, wetlands, and marine ecosystems. These environments are critical in mitigating the impacts of climate change and strengthening ecosystem stability.

Projects that protect habitats capable of long-term carbon storage or that help ecosystems adapt to changing climate conditions are particularly encouraged.

Community Engagement and Local Stewardship

A key component of EOCA-funded projects is community involvement. Conservation efforts are more sustainable when local communities actively participate in protecting and managing natural resources.

Projects applying for funding should demonstrate strong engagement with local populations and promote environmental stewardship through education and awareness initiatives.

EOCA prioritizes projects that:

  • Involve local communities in conservation activities

  • Promote sustainable resource management

  • Provide long-term environmental and social benefits

  • Strengthen local capacity for conservation

  • Encourage environmental education and awareness

By integrating local knowledge and community participation, conservation projects can achieve more sustainable and lasting environmental outcomes.

Project Eligibility Requirements

To qualify for EOCA funding, proposed projects must meet several important criteria.

Eligible projects must:

  • Protect a threatened species, habitat, or ecosystem

  • Demonstrate hands-on conservation activities

  • Address the root causes of biodiversity loss

  • Deliver measurable environmental outcomes

  • Promote community engagement and environmental stewardship

Practical conservation activities may include:

  • Habitat restoration

  • Removal of invasive species

  • Wildlife protection initiatives

  • Anti-poaching actions

  • Ecosystem restoration efforts

  • Biodiversity monitoring and protection programmes

Projects must focus on practical implementation rather than purely academic research.

Community and Rights-Based Approach

EOCA places strong emphasis on conservation initiatives that respect the rights of local communities and Indigenous peoples. Applicants should demonstrate that their projects consider social inclusion, local governance, and community rights.

Successful applications should clearly outline:

  • Participation of local communities in project planning and implementation

  • Respect for Indigenous and local stakeholder rights

  • Educational initiatives promoting conservation awareness

  • Long-term sustainability of conservation activities

Projects that create both environmental and social benefits are highly encouraged.

Ineligible Projects

Certain types of projects are not eligible for EOCA funding. These include:

  • Urban conservation projects

  • Pure academic research or scientific studies

  • Projects focused only on education or awareness

  • Infrastructure construction projects

  • Renewable energy installation initiatives

  • Waste management projects unrelated to biodiversity protection

  • Animal welfare or rehabilitation programmes for captive animals

  • Political or religious activities

  • General organisational operating costs

Applicants should ensure that their projects focus on practical biodiversity conservation and deliver measurable environmental outcomes.

Application and Selection Process

The EOCA funding programme uses a multi-stage evaluation process to ensure that selected projects have strong environmental impact and feasibility.

The selection process includes the following stages:

Stage 1: Initial Application

Applicants must first submit a Short Stage 1 Application Form outlining the proposed conservation project, its objectives, and expected outcomes.

Stage 2: Full Application

Applicants whose proposals pass the initial screening will be invited to submit a detailed full application. This stage requires more comprehensive information about the project implementation plan and conservation impact.

Scientific Review

Applications are evaluated by EOCA’s scientific advisory panel, which assesses the ecological relevance and feasibility of each project.

Final Selection and Public Voting

Shortlisted projects will participate in a two-week global public voting campaign. During this stage, organisations can mobilise supporters and raise awareness about their conservation initiatives.

The final selection of projects is based on:

  • Scientific review

  • EOCA member voting

  • Public voting results

This participatory approach allows conservation organisations to engage global audiences and build support for biodiversity protection.

Application Deadline

All applications must be submitted before the official deadline of 31 March 2026 at 12:00 Midnight (BST / GMT+1).

Applications submitted after this deadline will not be considered.

Organizations interested in applying should begin preparing their proposals early to ensure they meet all eligibility criteria and submission requirements.

Why This Funding Opportunity Matters

The EOCA Conservation Project Funding Call plays a crucial role in supporting grassroots conservation initiatives worldwide. By providing financial support and international visibility, the programme helps environmental organisations implement projects that protect biodiversity, restore degraded ecosystems, and empower communities.

In an era of accelerating biodiversity loss and climate change, initiatives like EOCA funding contribute to preserving natural ecosystems and building resilient environmental futures.

Organizations working in biodiversity conservation, habitat restoration, and community-based environmental protection are strongly encouraged to apply and contribute to global conservation efforts.

For more information click here  

Discover more opportunities here 

The post EOCA Conservation Project Funding Call 2026: Up to €30,000 for Global Biodiversity Protection and Community-Based Conservation Projects appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Internews Earth Journalism Network Grant for Clean Energy Reporting in India (Up to ₹3 Lakh Support)
item.dc:creator: Maria Ramirez
item.description:
Communicators and social media creators in India now have a unique opportunity to receive financial and editorial support to produce impactful stories about the country’s renewable energy transition. The Earth Journalism Network (EJN), an initiative of Internews, is inviting applications from journalists, communicators, and digital content creators to develop in-depth reporting projects focused on India’s […]

The post Internews Earth Journalism Network Grant for Clean Energy Reporting in India (Up to ₹3 Lakh Support) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Communicators and social media creators in India now have a unique opportunity to receive financial and editorial support to produce impactful stories about the country’s renewable energy transition.

The Earth Journalism Network (EJN), an initiative of Internews, is inviting applications from journalists, communicators, and digital content creators to develop in-depth reporting projects focused on India’s shift toward clean energy and clean mobility. Selected applicants will receive grant funding and one-on-one editorial mentorship to produce high-quality reports that highlight the progress, challenges, and solutions shaping India’s renewable energy future.

The application deadline is April 15, 2026.

Why This Grant Matters

India is rapidly transitioning toward renewable energy, with a national target of 500 GW renewable energy capacity by 2030. While progress is being made, several challenges remain, including delays in payments to renewable energy firms, limited grid capacity, land constraints for new projects, and uneven adoption of rooftop solar systems.

Through this grant program, EJN aims to support compelling reporting that informs the public, encourages policy dialogue, and highlights scalable solutions that empower local communities through decentralized renewable energy initiatives.

The program also seeks to promote strong storytelling and audience engagement through both traditional journalism and social media content.

What the Grant Supports

Selected applicants will receive funding and mentorship to produce original content focused on renewable energy in India.

Expected Outputs

For Communicators

  • One in-depth report or a series of reports

  • Formats may include text, video, or multimedia storytelling

For Social Media Content Creators

  • At least five engaging pieces of social media content related to the topic

Collaborative applications between communicators and content creators are strongly encouraged, though the application must be submitted by a single lead applicant who will manage communication and funding.

Funding and Budget

Applicants must submit a detailed budget outlining the costs required to produce their reporting project.

Funding guidelines include:

  • Up to ₹1 lakh for text or multimedia reporting projects

  • Up to ₹3 lakh for video reporting or collaborative projects between communicators and content creators

Budgets may include expenses such as travel, accommodation, translation, data visualization, and reporting-related costs. However, equipment purchases should not exceed 10% of the total budget.

Freelancers may include compensation for their work, provided it does not exceed 30% of the total budget.

Editorial Mentorship
